coredata.dc:title: Promoting the transfer of phosphorescence from the solid state to aqueous phase and establishing the universal real-time detection based on the smartphone imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Ying
Feng, Zhiying
Yan, Zihao
Yang, Xiaoming
coredata.dc:description: Currently, room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials inspire the great interest owing to their tunable wavelengths, large stokes shift or potential values towards various applications. Howbeit, synthesizing RTP materials with the desirable property such as the long lifetime still encountered the intractable challenge due to the spin-forbidden nature of triplet exciton transitions. Here we initially achieved one kind of solid state phosphorescence by preparing the carbon dots (CDs) doped with nitrogen and phosphorus while diethanolamine and phosphoric acid served as the specific precursors. Significantly, the CDs were effectively protected away from the interference of water and oxygen in the surrounding environment through introducing tetraethyl orthosilicate, thus leading to the successful emergence of their phosphorescence in aqueous. This study not only provided a way of activating the phosphorescent emission from the solid state to aqueous phase, but also broadened the sensing avenues based on the aqueous phosphorescence. Since both their fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity of the CDs were regularly quenched based on the inner filter effect (IFE) by adding p-nitrophenol with various concentrations, we thereupon proposed a regular dual-channel strategy for detecting p-nitrophenol. More meaningfully, both the Hue and Brightness of their fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs exhibited obvious distinctions, providing the possibility of establishing a dual-channel smartphone assaying strategy through directly photographing. Thus, we hereby raised a convenient intelligent detection of p-nitrophenol, and this brand-new way not only achieved the real-time detection of p-nitrophenol, but also showed the possibility of serving as a universal approach for detecting other targets on-site.

head.ce:title: Promoting the transfer of phosphorescence from the solid state to aqueous phase and establishing the universal real-time detection based on the smartphone imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, room-temperature phosphorescent (RTP) materials inspire the great interest owing to their tunable wavelengths, large stokes shift or potential values towards various applications. Howbeit, synthesizing RTP materials with the desirable property such as the long lifetime still encountered the intractable challenge due to the spin-forbidden nature of triplet exciton transitions. Here we initially achieved one kind of solid state phosphorescence by preparing the carbon dots (CDs) doped with nitrogen and phosphorus while diethanolamine and phosphoric acid served as the specific precursors. Significantly, the CDs were effectively protected away from the interference of water and oxygen in the surrounding environment through introducing tetraethyl orthosilicate, thus leading to the successful emergence of their phosphorescence in aqueous. This study not only provided a way of activating the phosphorescent emission from the solid state to aqueous phase, but also broadened the sensing avenues based on the aqueous phosphorescence. Since both their fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity of the CDs were regularly quenched based on the inner filter effect (IFE) by adding p-nitrophenol with various concentrations, we thereupon proposed a regular dual-channel strategy for detecting p-nitrophenol. More meaningfully, both the Hue and Brightness of their fluorescence and phosphorescence of CDs exhibited obvious distinctions, providing the possibility of establishing a dual-channel smartphone assaying strategy through directly photographing. Thus, we hereby raised a convenient intelligent detection of p-nitrophenol, and this brand-new way not only achieved the real-time detection of p-nitrophenol, but also showed the possibility of serving as a universal approach for detecting other targets on-site.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: During the early, the materials with room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) are striking owing to their bright prospects in the fields of chemical sensing [1,2], bioimaging [3–5], cancer diagnosis and treatment [5–7], anti-counterfeiting [8], optoelectronic devices [9–11] and beyond. Nevertheless, the current RTP materials mainly consist of the organic compounds and metal complexes, which still suffer several intrinsic defects including the high toxicity, short lifetime and rigorous synthesis conditions. Meanwhile, the triplet excited states of RTP materials are well-known as unstable, which could be easily quenched in various conditions such as water or dissolved oxygen [1,12]. Clearly, these factors result in their restricted applications of RTP materials. Hence, it is highly necessary to develop the innovative RTP materials with the improved properties like metal-free, low toxicity, cost effective, long lifetime and maintaining stable in the aqueous phase [13–15].

Usually, the following conventional strategies are raised to achieve the effective RTP. First, the introduction of halogens [16], aromatic carbonyl groups [17] or transition metals [18,19] could enhance the degree of the spin-orbit coupling (SOC), thus boosting intersystem crossing (ISC) from the excited singlet state to triplet state. Secondly, establishing a rigid environment could stabilize the excited triplet state and inhibit the non-radiative transitions [20], thereby promoting the occurrence of RTP. More specifically, the vibration and rotation of molecules are constrained by introducing the appropriate matrices, thus forming hydrogen bonds [14] or covalent bonds between each other [21].

Carbon dots (CDs) stand out from the family of luminescent nanomaterials on account of their tunable optical property [22], photobleaching resistance [23,24], low toxicity, small size, favorable biocompatibility [24,25] and cost-effective synthesis. Along with the enormous explorations by researchers, the synthesis methodology of CDs gradually developed into maturity. Up to date, various approaches have been raised to prepare diverse functional CDs, such as the hydrothermal method [26], microwave-assisted synthesis [27], laser ablation, electrochemical or chemical oxidation approach. According to their particular functional properties, CDs show the potential applications for the chemical sensing [28,29], biological imaging [30], optoelectronic applying [31,32], information encrypting [33–35], and more.

Interestingly, the RTP phenomenon of CDs was captured by dispersing CDs into the matrix of polyvinyl alcohol when excited with ultraviolet (UV) light, and the phosphorescent CDs were thereupon defined for the first time [36]. However, the phosphorescence of CDs could be easily quenched by water or dissolved oxygen, and CDs-based RTP materials are mainly utilized as the solid form, thus hindering the RTP of CDs from being exploited to the greatest extent. Therefore, it is valuable and challengeable to devote contributions to synthesizing CD-based RTP materials, which could show the stable phosphorescent emission not only in solid state, but also in aqueous state. Recently, the strategies of emitting the phosphorescence for carbon dots in aqueous have been raised. For instance, the aqueous dispersion of CDs@MP [37] exhibited the yellow RTP both in solid and aqueous forms via the molten salt method, while the inorganic salts (KNO3, MgCl2, and KH2PO4) and carbon sources (1,2,4-triaminobenzene) were directly calcinated. Again, silane compounds [12], melamine [38] or other substances were introduced to form the hydrogen and covalent bonds with the carbon dots, thus stabilizing their triplet states and protecting the phosphorescence in the aqueous.

p-Nitrophenol, being involved in the production of pharmaceuticals, pesticides, dyes and leather [39,40], plays a critical and indispensable role in chemical manufacturing. However, a mass of evidence has been raised to prove the existence of p-nitrophenol in industrial and agricultural effluent with the ever-rising demand of mankind, revealing the increasingly severe water pollution. As is well known, p-nitrophenol in water, even at a low concentration, could greatly reduce the output of crops [41]. More seriously, the abuse and non-standard disposal of p-nitrophenol may induce the fearful damage because of its strong carcinogenicity, toxicity, mutagenicity and recalcitrance [41,42]. For instance, over-exposure to p-nitrophenol could give rise to the eye and skin irritation, liver and kidney damage, methemoglobin forming, anemia and systemic poisoning [39]. Hence, the large amount of p-nitrophenol pollutes our water on the earth, thus severely increasing the environmental problems and threatening people's health.

Although diverse approaches have been developed for detecting p-nitrophenol such as the high performance liquid chromatography, electrochemical analysis [43,44] or capillary electrophoresis [45], these methods are usually considered to be laborious and hardly achieve the portable and real-time detections. Also, there emerged the fluorescent detection for p-nitrophenol through the regular CDs [46]. However, this kind of single-channel detection, which only relies on the fluorescence, usually shows the disadvantage of the narrow detection range and low sensitivity. Thereby, developing the dual-channel assaying of p-nitrophenol not only facilitates the detecting results to be more accurate and reliable, but also greatly covers the shortage of the single-channel strategy. During the past decade, the fluorescent CDs have been extensively studied, but synthesizing the CDs with RTP is still challengeable. Generally, the conventional dual-channel sensing requires the employment of the fluorescence spectrophotometer, but it is inconvenient to be carried around due to its large size and weight, and thus the timely results could not be obtained on site. Therefore, developing the real-time detections based on the portable instruments has been considered as the key point in the analytical field. Overall, it is of great novelty and value to combine the dual-channel method with the portable device in the field of real-time detections, especially p-nitrophenol in environmental waters.

In the present study, we proposed a rapid strategy for the synthesis of CDs with both the fluorescence and room temperature solid-state phosphorescence (S-CDs). In brief, diethanolamine and phosphoric acid served as the precursors for preparing the RTP CDs through the microwave-assisted heating, and its corresponding aqueous solution showed the bright-blue fluorescence, while the green phosphorescence was observed on the filter paper loaded with S-CDs. By virtue of its satisfactory luminescent property, we thereupon built up a sensitive and regular dual-channel assay of p-nitrophenol. More significantly, we achieved the aqueous phosphorescence of CDs (A-CDs) by introducing tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS). In this way, we established the mode of sensing the targets with both the fluorescence and phosphorescence in the aqueous phase, whereas the routine solid phosphorescent detections usually require a long-time drying procedure. Towards achieving the efficient real-time detection, we have further raised an intelligent image sensing mode by employing the smartphones. In particular, the fluorescent and phosphorescent colors of the samples were extracted from the images by photographing, and their numerical key parameters were input into the application (app) of smartphone. Hence, we could obtain the output results within 30 s, and the dual-channel intelligent strategy was successfully established on the basis of the smartphone imaging. Additionally, we also broadened the application of the current CDs functioning as the fluorescent and phosphorescent ink for the encryption and decryption of information ( Scheme 1).

Basically, diethanolamine (99%), 4-nitrochlorobenzene (99%), nicotinamide (99%), L-tryptophan (99%), chloramphenicol (98%), sulfamethoxazole (98%), tetraethyl orthosilicate (98%) and aqueous ammonia (25–28%) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China), while gentamicin (USP, 590 u/mg) and acyclovir (99%) were both obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Again, metronidazole (99%) and quinine sulphate (98%) were acquired from Meryer Chemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Also, clindamycin (98%) was bought from ShangHai D&B Biological Science and Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) as well as ribavirin (98%) from Bide Pharmatech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Meanwhile, BaCl20.2 H2O (AR, 99%), CoCl20.6 H2O (98%), AlCl30.6 H2O (99%), CuCl20.2 H2O (99%), FeCl30.6 H2O (99%), KCl (99.8%), MnCl20.6 H2O (99%), NaCl (99.5%), NiSO40.6 H2O (AR, 98.5%), Pb(CH3COO)20.3 H2O (99.5%), SrCl20.6 H2O (99.5%), dimethyl sulfoxide (99.5%), ethanol (99.5%), acetonitrile (≥99.5%), formaldehyde (AR, 37–40%), acetaldehyde (AR, 40%), formic acid (≥99%), acetone (99.5%), boric acid (AR, ≥99.5%) and glacial acetic acid (99.8%) were purchased from Chron Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Chengdu, China). Likewise, phosphoric acid (AR, ≥85 wt% in H2O), p-nitrophenol (99%), m-nitrophenol (99%), o-nitrophenol (99%), N, N-dimethylformamide tetrahydrofuran (99.8%) and sodium hydroxide (98%) were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). In addition, Aquapro AWL-0502-P ultrapure water system (Chongqing, China) provided the ultrapure water for the whole experiment process, and Britton Robinson (BR) buffer solution was utilized to adjust the pH values of the solutions. Generally, the previous reagents were directly employed without further purification.

All the fluorescent and phosphorescent assays were accomplished with a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan) at room temperature, and the UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by using a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Simultaneously, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were acquired by a TECNAI G2 F20 microscope (Columbia, America) at 200 kV, while the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by a Rigaku Ministar instrument (Tokyo, Japan). Next, the elements and functional groups of CDs were clarified through an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (New York, America) and a Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). Meanwhile, the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes of CDs were respectively measured at the excitation of 403 nm and 353 nm by an Edinburgh FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (Paris, France). Further, the related powder of CDs was collected through lyophilization by PiloFD8–4.3 V (Charlotte, USA). Additionally, the diverse reaction temperatures were regulated by a water bath (DF-101 s) of Gongyi experimental instruments factory (Gongyi, China), and various pH values of the solutions were measured by a Fangzhong pHS-3 C digital pH meter (Chengdu, China). Again, all the mixtures were separated by the centrifuge (TG16-W) of Cence instrument Co., Ltd. (Changsha, China). Also, the microwave oven of Galanz (Shunde, China) was utilized to synthesize CDs, and the electric heating drying oven of (DHG-9140A) from Keelrein instrument Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) served for drying the filter paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the solid-state phosphorescent CDs (S-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: To begin this process, 6 mL of phosphoric acid was added into 3.0 mL of diethanolamine dropwise, and this liquid was continuously stirred until no more water vapor escaped, exhibiting the color of inconspicuous pink. Subsequently, the previous solution was dissolved in 16 mL of ultrapure water, and its color turned to be transparent again. Next, this solution was transferred into the crucible followed by heating in a microwave oven over medium-high heat for 3 min, and the mixture changed to be a gel-like solid. After cooling down to the room temperature, the suspension of CDs was dissolved by 25 mL of ultrapure water followed by adjusting its pH to be neutral with the solution of NaOH (18 mol/L). Then, the coarse particles were removed by centrifugation, and the supernatant was thereupon collected. The supernatant was filtered through the filter paper, and further subjected to 0.22 µm of microporous membrane to remove the residual macromolecular impurities. The final liquid obtained was dialyzed at 25 °C for 8 h with a MWCO of 300, and thus the light yellow powder of the solid-state phosphorescent CDs (S-CDs) was acquired through the lyophilization, which was stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the aqueous-phase phosphorescent CDs (A-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Typically, 18 mL of ultrapure water and 1 mL of filtered S-CDs solution were successively added into 50 mL of round-bottom flask. When the S-CDs dispersed evenly, 2.5 mL of tetraethyl orthosilicate and 0.125 mL of ammonia were separately introduced. Subsequently, the flask was transferred into the preheated water bath, and this reaction was incubated with continuous stirring at 60 °C for 6 h to obtain the aqueous-phase phosphorescent CDs (A-CDs). Then, the solution obtained here was subjected to the centrifugation at 4500 rpm for 5 min to remove the suspended pellet, and thus the supernatant was collected. Through this way, A-CDs that showed the phosphorescence in aqueous phase was successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of p-nitrophenol by fluorescence and solid-phosphorescence
ce:section.ce:para: During the process of detecting p-nitrophenol with fluorescence, 100 μL of S-CDs solution (20 mg/mL) and 100 μL of p-nitrophenol with varied concentrations were respectively added into the parallel eppendorf (EP) tubes of 1.50 mL followed by diluted to 1 mL with britton-robinson (BR) buffer solution (pH=7). After the incubation at 50 °C for 20 min, their fluorescence intensity were measured with the excitation of 403 nm. As for the phosphorescence sensing, the procedure was similar to that of the fluorescence detection. Likewise, 100 μL of S-CDs solution was pipetted into multiple EP tubes of 1.5 mL, then 100 μL of different concentrations of p-nitrophenol and 800 μL of BR buffer (pH=7) were sequentially introduced to these mixtures to reach a total volume of 1 mL. Further, 50 μL of the previous solutions were dropped onto each filter paper (1.2 ×1.3 cm), and their related phosphorescence were instantly recorded after being completely dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference for S-CDs detecting p-nitrophenol
ce:section.ce:para: For investigating the selectivity of S-CDs assaying p-nitrophenol, 100 μL of S-CDs and 800 μL of BR buffer (pH=7) were parallelly introduced into the EP tubes of 1.5 mL. Next, 100 μL of various related substances including p-nitrophenol, 4-nitrochlorobenzene, ribavirin, nicotinamide, L-tryptophan, acyclovir, chloramphenicol, sulfamethoxazole, gentamicin, catechol, resorcinol, hydroquinone, m-nitrophenol and o-nitrophenol (10-3 mol/L) were respectively added into the prepared mixtures followed by thorough mixing. Following the incubation at 50 °C for 20 min, their fluorescence intensities were measured in solutions, while their phosphorescence were recorded on paper after being completely dried.

During the procedure of testing the potential interference, 100 μL of S-CDs, 100 μL of p-nitrophenol, 700 μL of BR buffer (pH=7) and 100 μL of various substances (4-nitrochlorobenzene, ribavirin, nicotinamide, L-tryptophan, acyclovir, chloramphenicol, sulfamethoxazole, gentamicin, catechol, resorcinol, hydroquinone, m-nitrophenol and o-nitrophenol, each for 10-3 mol/L) were successively added into the EP tubes, and were mixed thoroughly. By performing the similar treatment previously described, their fluorescence and phosphorescence intensity were measured, respectively.

To obtain the superior luminescent performance of S-CDs, the synthesis conditions were optimized through a series of experiments, and the phosphorescence variation of S-CDs was initially investigated. As shown in Fig. 1A, S-CDs exhibited the strongest phosphorescence with the reaction time of 3 min. Because S-CDs could not be sufficiently carbonized with the reaction time of 1 or 2 min, leading to their low phosphorescence intensity, and S-CDs of excessive carbonization with more than 4 min also emitted the similar weak phosphorescence. Subsequently, the amount ratio of diethanolamine to phosphoric acid was explored, and S-CDs showed the highest phosphorescence intensity with the ratio of 1:2 (Fig. 1B). Similarly, we also verified the fluorescence property of S-CDs with these conditions (Fig. S1), which exhibited the same variation trend as the phosphorescence intensity. Therefore, 3 mL of diethanolamine, 6 mL of phosphoric acid and 3 min were determined as the optimal conditions for preparing S-CDs.

Upon the optimized conditions, the solution of S-CDs showed as light yellow under daylight, which emitted the blue bright fluorescence emission of 441 nm with an excitation of 403 nm (Fig. 1C and D). Further, S-CDs exhibited the green phosphorescence while being loaded on the filter paper, and its excitation and emission wavelengths were 353 nm and 506 nm, respectively (Fig. 1E and F). Subsequently, the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetime of S-CDs were calculated as 7.21 ns and 1353.90 ms (Table S1 and S2) through their decay spectra (Fig. S2), revealing its generally satisfactory luminescent property. Again, the fluorescence emissions of S-CDs presented the red-shift trend accompanied by the excitation wavelength rising (Fig. S3A), describing its excitation-dependent nature, but its phosphorescence emissions were hardly affected by the various excitations (Fig. S3B).

To gain insights into S-CDs, we performed the systematic characterizations. To be specific, the transmission electron microscope (TEM) image indicated that S-CDs appeared as the regular spheres with the diameter of less than 5 nm (Fig. 1G). Meanwhile, the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was also clearly captured (Fig. 1G, inset), which corresponded to the (100) facet of graphite, indicating its graphite structure of S-CDs [47,48]. Next, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were employed in combination to identify the functional groups and elemental composition of S-CDs. Briefly, three peaks at 3415 cm-1, 3242 cm-1 and 2872 cm-1 (Fig. 1H) were originated from the stretching vibrations of -OH, -NH2 and -CH2- [14,31], while the peak from 2540 cm-1 to 2300 cm-1 was attributed to the bending vibration of P-OH [49]. Importantly, the peak of 1650 cm-1 was closely related to the group of CN [14], which might serve as the photoluminescence core of S-CDs. Also, the peak at 1466 cm-1 was derived from the bending vibration of -CH2- [14,50]. Again, the stretching vibration peaks at 1272 cm-1, 1092 cm-1, 873 cm-1 and 520 cm-1 described the formation of the functional groups including PO, P-O-C, P-N and PO4 3- [14,49–52]. Furthermore, XPS provided the evidence for the composition of S-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 1I, S-CDs mainly consisted of four elements including P (15%), C (50.73%), N (4.36%) and O (29.92%). Specifically, the curve of C 1 s was fitted as two peaks of 284.85 eV and 286.34 eV, which were derived from C-C/CC and C-O/C-N/C-P (Fig. 1J) [52]. Still, the high-resolution XPS survey of N 1 s indicated the existence of C-NC/N-(C)3 and N-H/N-P at 399.7 eV and 401.2 eV (Fig. 1K) [14,51]. Again, the three peaks of O 1 s at 530.66 eV, 532.46 eV and 535.49 eV were associated with the functional groups of PO, C-O-C and P-O (Fig. 1L) [14,31], while the two peaks of P 2p spectra were separately assigned to P-O (133.34 eV) and P-C/P-N (134.10 eV) (Fig. 1M) [52]. Taken together, these findings from XPS described a high agreement with that through FTIR.

As shown in Fig. 1N, the XRD pattern of S-CDs showed a peak at around 26°. According to the Bragg equation, the interlayer spacing was calculated to be 0.34 nm, belonging to the (002) plane of graphite [48]. In the UV–vis spectrum of S-CDs, there existed two absorption peaks at 260 nm and 353 nm, respectively (Fig. 1O). In brief, the former peak was attributed to the π-π * transition of CC, and the latter one stemmed from the n-π * transition of CN [53,54], providing the evidence for its luminescence of S-CDs.

To address whether the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs remains stable under different conditions or not, we investigated the possible effect of various temperatures, pH, concentrations of NaCl, time, organic solvents, and metal ions on its fluorescence.

Basically, the fluorescence of S-CDs scarcely varied over the temperature range of 20–90 °C (Fig. S4A). As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs kept stable for a wide range of pH (Fig. S4B), describing its strong ability of acid-base resistance. Similarly, either the various concentrations of NaCl (0–1.2 mol/L) or the long exposure time up to 120 min barely affected its fluorescence, revealing the favorable salt and photobleaching tolerance of S-CDs (Fig. S4C and D). Likewise, the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs hardly showed the decrease or enhancement in diverse organic solvents and metal ions (Fig. S4E and F). Thus, these results demonstrated that the fluorescence of S-CDs was rarely interfered by the external conditions, proving its advanced fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence/RTP property and application of S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As described in Fig. 2A, the green phosphorescence of S-CDs was observed for up to 10 s with the naked eye once the UV lamp was switched off, indicating its potential of functioning as the luminescent ink. In brief, we dipped the S-CDs solution with a brush to draw the pattern of a panda on the sketch paper (Fig. 2B). Similarly, the application of S-CDs as the optical ink was explored by drawing the logo of WHO and four artistic Chinese characters including spring, summer, autumn and winter (Fig. 2C). These patterns initially appeared as pale yellow under the sunlight, and showed the blue fluorescence when exposed to the UV lamp of 365 nm, then exhibited the obvious green phosphorescence with UV off, revealing the advantage of S-CDs as the luminescent ink.

Towards the information encryption, S-CDs together with a non-RTP ink was employed to encrypt the real information referring to the Caesar cipher (Fig. 2D). Basically, S-CDs served for conveying the real information, while the non-RTP ink was applied as the interferent, thus the true information could not be deciphered either with daylight or under UV. In this regard, the string of characters “8SHADEFOH8” was written with two kinds of inks mentioned previously, whereas only “3SHDFH” was written by the RTP ink of S-CDs. According to the rule of Caesar ciphers, the number of "3" on the left meant that all the remaining letters moved backward by three letters in alphabetical order, and thus the real information was decoded as "PEACE". Analogously, "LOVE" was cracked in the same way, while the right number of "6" represented that each letter moved forward by six letters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizing the conditions of S-CDs detecting p-nitrophenol
ce:section.ce:para: To build up the accurate detection of p-nitrophenol by S-CDs, the possible effect of pH, temperature or incubation time on this assay was explored. Obviously, the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs in presence of p-nitrophenol decreased with pH increasing (Fig. S5A), revealing that its fluorescence quenching by p-nitrophenol was pH-dependent. As shown in Fig. S5B, the quenching efficiency slightly varied for different temperatures, and a mild temperature of 50 °C was selected. Again, p-nitrophenol displayed the maximum quenching effect with the incubation time of 20 min (Fig. S5C). Hence, pH= 7, 50 °C and 20 min served as the optimal conditions for detecting p-nitrophenol by S-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dual-channel detection of p-nitrophenol by S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Through optimizing the detecting conditions, a dual-channel strategy of assaying p-nitrophenol by S-CDs was successfully established. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs in presence of p-nitrophenol decreased compared with that of S-CDs, agreeing with the corresponding images (Fig. 3A, inset). Accordingly, the linear relationship between the concentrations of p-nitrophenol (0.04–100 μM) and the fluorescence intensity of S-CDs was built up (Fig. 3B). Meanwhile, the regression equation was fitted as F0/F = 0.0159 C+ 1.030 with the coefficient of R2 = 0.986, while the detection limit was calculated to be 0.036 μM at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3 (Fig. 3C). Similarly, the phosphorescence intensity of S-CDs also showed the decrease as its fluorescence with the concentration of p-nitrophenol rising from 1 to 8000 μM (Fig. 3D and E). The related linear equation was plotted as P0/P = 3.234 C+ 1.174 with the coefficient of R2 = 0.986 and the detection limit of 0.866 μM (Fig. 3F). Taken together, the linear range of the fluorescent assay (0.04–100 μM) was applicable for sensing p-nitrophenol with the lower concentrations, while the phosphorescence-dependent method (1–8000 μM) was suitable for detecting the higher concentrations. These two sensing modes showed complementary to each other, and achieved the wider detection range of p-nitrophenol, thus promoting its preponderance of the dual-channel detection described here.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of S-CDs detecting p-nitrophenol
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of S-CDs detecting p-nitrophenol, we measured the FL and Phos intensity of S-CDs while coexisting with different substances including p-nitrophenol, 4-nitrochlorobenzene, ribavirin, nicotinamide, L-tryptophan, acyclovir, chloramphenicol, sulfamethoxazole, gentamicin, catechol, resorcinol, hydroquinone, m-nitrophenol and o-nitrophenol (Fig. S6A and C). These results indicated that both the fluorescence and phosphorescence of S-CDs were obviously quenched by p-nitrophenol, and the introductions of other chemicals hardly affected the luminescence of S-CDs, demonstrating the generally satisfactory selectivity of S-CDs detecting p-nitrophenol. To test the anti-interference ability of S-CDs, we measured the FL and Phos intensity of the mixture with introducing the additional interfering drugs, in which there existed S-CDs and p-nitrophenol (Fig. S6B and D). Obviously, the fluorescence and phosphorescence of S-CDs with both p-nitrophenol and the interfering substance hardly changed compared with that of S-CDs containing p-nitrophenol only, revealing its favorable anti-interference for assaying p-nitrophenol.

To understand the cause of p-nitrophenol quenching its fluorescence and phosphorescence of S-CDs, a series of experiments were designed and performed. Initially, the possible mechanisms of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and Electron transfer (ET) were studied. Since the UV–vis spectrum of p-nitrophenol hardly overlapped with the FL/Phos emission of S-CDs, FRET could be preliminarily excluded here [55]. Meanwhile, the fluorescence and phosphorescence lifetimes of S-CDs with the introduction of p-nitrophenol were 7.35 ns and 1340.76 ms (Table S1 and S2), which were similar to that of S-CDs without p-nitrophenol, revealing that p-nitrophenol scarcely affected its luminescent lifetimes (Fig. S7A and B). Thus, it was further confirmed that FRET was not responsible for regulating the quenching of S-CDs. Again, the Zeta potentials of S-CDs and p-nitrophenol were measured as − 7.01 mV and − 2.06 mV, indicating the existence of the electrostatic repulsion between each other, thus the effect of electron transfer was ruled out [56].

To deeply elucidate the quenching process, we analyzed the UV–vis spectra of p-nitrophenol, S-CDs or S-CDs with p-nitrophenol at different pH of 2, 7 or 12 in detail. Obviously, it was observed that the absorption peak of p-nitrophenol showed the bathochromic shift accompanied by pH increasing. The absorption peaks respectively emerged at 305 nm and 399.5 nm with the acidic and alkaline conditions, whereas there displayed two peaks at 319.5 nm and 400.6 nm simultaneously for the neutral pH (Fig. S8A). Simultaneously, varied pH scarcely affected the UV–vis spectra of S-CDs (Fig. S8B), while the absorption peaks of S-CDs with p-nitrophenol were interfered with pH changes (Fig. S8C). More specifically, the UV–vis spectrum of S-CDs with p-nitrophenol was highly consistent with that of p-nitrophenol or S-CDs only at the corresponding pH, indicating that there was no new peak appeared here (Fig. S8D, E and F). Likewise, the infrared spectra of S-CDs without or with p-nitrophenol indicated that no new compound formed based on the same peak shapes (Fig. S9A), which was consistent with the data of the UV spectra. Additionally, the overlap between the absorption spectra of p-nitrophenol and the fluorescence excitations spectra of S-CDs gradually expanded with pH rising ( Fig. 4A and B), and eventually the two peaks almost coincided (Fig. 4C). Therefore, the inner filter effect (IFE) was mainly responsible for the fluorescent response of S-CDs to p-nitrophenol. This finding also confirmed that the fluorescence quenching of S-CDs by p-nitrophenol regularly grew as pH increased (Fig. S9B). Usually, the phosphorescence of the luminophores is closely associated with its fluorescence, and the overlap of the phosphorescence excitation spectra for S-CDs with the absorption peak of p-nitrophenol also expanded by pH rising (Fig. 4D, E and F), resulting in the phosphorescence intensity of S-CDs with p-nitrophenol gradually decreased (Fig. S9C), which showed an agreement with that of its fluorescence.

Furthermore, we explored the possibility of S-CDs distinguishing p-nitrophenol, o-nitrophenol and m-nitrophenol. Compared with p-nitrophenol, either o-nitrophenol or m-nitrophenol hardly affected the fluorescence of S-CDs, suggesting that its fluorescence only responded to p-nitrophenol here (Fig. S10A). In contrast, o-nitrophenol enhanced the phosphorescence intensity of S-CDs, whereas m-nitrophenol showed a negligible effect, thus the three chemicals with similar structures were distinguished (Fig. S10B). To clarify the reason why S-CDs only responded to p-nitrophenol among the three, the UV spectra of o-nitrophenol and m-nitrophenol were obtained accordingly, revealing that both hardly overlapped with the excitation spectrum of S-CDs (Fig. S10C and D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Promoting S-CDs to emit the aqueous phosphorescence and its mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: To lead S-CDs to emit the phosphorescence in aqueous, further exploration has been contributed to the preparation of the aqueous-phase phosphorescence CDs (A-CDs) through introducing tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and ammonia water. During this process, TEOS reacted with water and S-CDs, while ammonia water was added to catalyze the reaction.

To be specific, the complete process mainly included three steps of the hydrolysis reaction, condensation reaction and polymerization reaction, among which the hydrolysis played the key role. During the initial stage, the hydrolysis of TEOS produced silicon hydroxide, and formed the covalent bonds (Si-O-C) and hydrogen bonds with S-CDs. Thereby, the formation of these bonds embedded S-CDs in the silica networks, and enhanced the rigidity of the crosslinked framework, which effectively inhibited the nonradiative dissipation, thus leading to the phosphorescence emerging in the aqueous phase ( Fig. 5 A).

As shown in Fig. S11A, different volumes of S-CDs solution (0.5–5 mL) were involved in the aqueous phosphorescence intensity, describing that A-CDs showed the maximum phosphorescence with 1 mL of S-CDs. Since the amounts of both TEOS and ammonia water played the critical role in the phosphorescence intensity of A-CDs, the appropriate ratios of the two substances were tested, and the optimal dosage of TEOS was selected as 2.5 mL as well as ammonia of 0.125 mL (Fig. S11B and C). Again, the corresponding reaction temperature and time were optimized. The aqueous phosphorescence exhibited the highest intensity at the reaction temperature of 60 ℃ (Fig. S11D). As for the reaction time, the phosphorescence intensity of A-CDs reached the strongest intensity for 6 h (Fig. S11E). Taking all the above into consideration, 1 mL of S-CDs solution, 2.5 mL of TEOS and 0.125 mL of ammonia water at 60 °C for 6 h were selected as the optimal reacting conditions for preparing A-CDs.

In this way, the aqueous phosphorescence CDs (A-CDs) were successfully prepared, showing the afterglow up to nine seconds (Fig. 5B). The solution of A-CDs exhibited as colorless and transparent under sunlight, while its fluorescence was bright blue as well as the phosphorescence showed green (Fig. 5C and D, inset a, b, c). Also, when the fluorescence and phosphorescence of A-CDs were excited by the maximum excitation of S-CDs, the luminescence of A-CDs showed similar high intensity.

To obtain a clearer and deeper understanding of the structure and the surface morphology of A-CDs, we further designed a series of characterizations of A-CDs by using transmission electron microscope (TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and UV–vis. As shown in Fig. S12A, the S-CDs were uniformly dispersed in the amorphous silica framework and anchored within the matrix, indicating the formation of A-CDs (CDs@SiO2). Next, FTIR and XPS were employed to explore the surface structure and elemental composition of A-CDs. As appeared in Fig. S12B, the absorption peak at 3423 cm-1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H from A-CDs [12], and the vibration of Si-O-C appeared at around 1218 cm-1 [57–59]. Importantly, the characteristic vibrations at 1105 and 800 cm-1 were originated from the asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching vibration of Si-O-Si, while the peak at 466 cm-1 was resulted from the bending vibration of Si-O-Si [57–61]. Again, the peak at 964 cm-1 was from the stretching vibrations of Si-OH [59].

According to the XPS results, A-CDs contained the elements of C (8.08%), N (0.85%), O (62.79%), Si (25.88%) and P (2.39%) (Fig. S12C) [31,58]. To be specific, the C 1 s band involved two peaks including C-C/CC (284.91 eV) and C-O/C-N/C-P (286.56 eV) (Fig. S12D) [52], while the two peaks of N 1 s at 400.09 eV and 401.84 eV were separately assigned to C-NC/N-(C)3 and N-H/N-P (Fig. S12E) [14,51]. Next, the curve of O 1 s was fitted into four peaks at 531.29 532.91, 533.70 and 535.33 eV, corresponding to PO, O-C-O, Si-O and P-O groups, respectively (Fig. S12F) [12,14,31,57,58]. The two peaks of Si 2p at 103.52 eV and 104.29 eV were associated with the functional groups of Si-O-C and Si-Ox (Fig. S12G) [12,62], indicating the existence of covalent bonds in A-CDs. Again, The P 2p band showed two peaks at 133.73 eV for P-O and 134.58 eV for P-C/P-N, respectively (Fig. S12H) [63]. Based on the results of FTIR and XPS, we concluded that the covalent bonds (Si-O-C) and hydrogen bonds formed between S-CDs and the silica matrix, which enhanced the rigidity of the crosslinked framework and inhibited the nonradiative dissipation and stabilized the triplet excitons, thus triggering the room temperature phosphorescence in the aqueous. Again, the UV–vis spectrum of A-CDs showed two absorption peaks at 262 nm and 337 nm (Fig. S12I), which was similar to the UV–vis absorption of S-CDs, further demonstrating that the luminescence of A-CDs was due to S-CDs embedded in the matrix of silica.

In addition, the fluorescent and phosphorescent stability of A-CDs under different conditions were investigated (Fig. S13 and S14), which both showed the generally desirable stability. Meanwhile, 20 °C and 20 min were optimized as the subsequent detection conditions (Fig. S15).

Along with the popularization and development of smartphones, there emerged diverse functional applications (apps), and the photos taken by their cameras were becoming clear enough, thus providing the possibility of the smartphones being applied for scientific research. Especially, the combination of photographing and the related apps shows the potentiality in the real-time detection.

In the current study, the luminescence of A-CDs displayed the regular change of hue and brightness with adding various concentrations of p-nitrophenol, which could be easily captured by the cameras. Accordingly, we developed a real-time strategy of detecting p-nitrophenol by combining the camera with two apps named Color ID and Universal Formula of the smartphones. In brief, A-CDs with diverse concentrations of p-nitrophenol were irradiated with the UV lamp, and their corresponding fluorescence was photographed with UV on ( Fig. 6A, the upper row), while the images of their phosphorescence were immediately captured with UV off (Fig. 6A, the bottom row), and these obtained photos of these solutions were imported into the app of Color ID (Fig. 6A). Subsequently, the detailed information about these colors, especially for Hue and Brightness, was output by clicking the color on the images (Fig. 6B). Based on this extracted information, the linear relationship between p-nitrophenol and H/B of A-CDs was rapidly established, where H and B referred to the values of Hue and Brightness for the images. Precisely, the linear relationship of assaying p-nitrophenol by the fluorescence (8 μM to 1000 μM) was fitted as H/B = 1.549 C + 0.545 with R2 of 0.987, and the detection limit was calculated to be 6.364 μM (Fig. 6C, a). Again, the linear relationship by the phosphorescence (8 μM to 7000 μM) was plotted as H/B = 1.444 C + 0.392 with R2 of 0.982 as well as the detection limit of 5.145 μM (Fig. 6C, b). To accelerate the real-time detection of p-nitrophenol, the formulas of the established linear relationships were converted into the automatic calculators through being saved into the app of Universal Formula (Fig. 6D). Next, the values of hue and brightness were directly input followed by clicking the button of Calculate, and the final results were output instantly (Fig. 6E and F). Overall, the whole process only cost around 30 s from photographing to acquiring the results, and thus this swift and convenient smartphone-image detecting strategy was successfully established.

On the whole, S-CDs with the blue fluorescence and green solid-state phosphorescence was obtained, and this S-CDs was employed as the fluorescent and phosphorescent ink for drawing various patterns and encrypting information. Importantly, the introduced TEOS formed the hydrogen bonds and covalent bonds with S-CDs through the hydrolytic polycondensation reaction, thus achieving the transfer of its phosphorescence from the solid-state to aqueous-phase (A-CDs). Meanwhile, both the fluorescence and phosphorescence of S/A-CDs were quenched with the addition of p-nitrophenol due to IFE, thus presenting different Hue and Brightness. Accordingly, we initially proposed a conventional dual-channel detection of p-nitrophenol by monitoring the luminescence intensity. More meaningfully, we further developed a smartphone-image assaying strategy through analyzing the fluorescent and phosphorescent changes (Hue and Brightness) with the functional apps. Therefore, this strategy dramatically accelerated the detection speed of p-nitrophenol and avoided the utilization of large instruments. More significantly, the designed smartphone-image assaying mode could serve as a universal method for the real-time detection of other targets.

Ying Zhu: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data processing, Original draft, Investigation. Zhiying Feng: Software, Validation. Zihao Yan: Review & editing. Xiaoming Yang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaoming Yang respectively obtained his B.S. degree in 2004 and Ph. D. degree in 2009 at Sichuan University, China. During 2007–2009, he stayed at Purdue University as a Joint Ph.D. for pursuing his study in the field of biochemistry. Since 2010, he has been serving as a full professor in College of Pharmaceutical Sciences of Southwest University, China. His research interests mainly focus on the phosphorescent carbon dots, metal nanoclusters and their related applications.
